title: Publications
text:
Iago N Mamede, Prashant Singh, Arnab Pal, C.E. Fiore and Karel Proesmans New Journal of Physics, Volume 26, March 2024
DOI | arXiv
We investigate the work fluctuations in an overdamped non-equilibrium process that is stopped at a stochastic time. The latter is characterized by a first passage event that marks the completion of the non-equilibrium process. In particular, we consider a particle diffusing in one dimension in the presence of a time-dependent potential \(U(x,t)=k|x−vt|n/n\), where \(k>0\) is the stiffness and \(n>0\) is the order of the potential. Moreover, the particle is confined between two absorbing walls, located at \(L\pm(t)\), that move with a constant velocity \(v\) and are initially located at \(L\pm(0)=\pm L\). As soon as the particle reaches any of the boundaries, the process is said to be completed and here, we compute the work done W by the particle in the modulated trap upto this random time. Employing the Feynman-Kac path integral approach, we find that the typical values of the work scale with \(L\) with a crucial dependence on the order \(n\). While for \(n>1\), we show that \(\langle{W}\rangle \sim L^{1-n}~\exp \left[ \left( {k L^{n}}/{n}-v L \right)/D \right]\) for large \(L\), we get an algebraic scaling of the form \(\langle{W}\rangle \sim L^n\) for the \(n<1\) case. The marginal case of \(n=1\) is exactly solvable and our analysis unravels three distinct scaling behaviours: (i) \(\rangle{W}\langle \sim L\) for \(v>k\), (ii) \(\langle{W}\rangle \sim L^2\) for \(v=k\) and (iii) \(\langle{W}\rangle \sim \exp\left[{-(v-k)L}\right]\) for \(v<k\). For all cases, we also obtain the probability distribution associated with the typical values of \(W\). Finally, we observe an interesting set of relations between the relative fluctuations of the work done and the first-passage time for different \(n\) – which we argue physically. Our results are well supported by the numerical simulations.




Iago Mamede, Karel Proesmans and C.E. Fiore
Phys. Rev. Research 5, 043278 – Published 22 December, 2023
DOI | arXiv
We study a class of system composed of interacting unicyclic machines placed in contact with a hot and cold thermal baths subjected to a nonconservative driving worksource. Despite their simplicity, these models showcase an intricate array of phenomena, including pump and heat engine regimes as well as a discontinuous phase transition. We look at three distinctive topologies: a minimal and beyond minimal (homogeneous and heterogeneous interaction structures). The former case is represented by stark different networks (“all-to-all” interactions and only a central interacting to its neighbors) and present exact solutions, whereas homogeneous and heterogeneous structures have been analyzed by numerical simulations. We find that the topology plays a major role on the thermodynamic performance for smaller values of individual energies, in part due to the presence of first-order phase transitions. Contrariwise, the topology becomes less important as individual energies increases and results are well described by a system with all-to-all interactions.




Iago Mamede, Angel L. L. Stable and C.E. Fiore
Phys. Rev. E 106, 064125 – Published 19 December, 2022
DOI | arXiv
Brownian particles interacting sequentially with distinct temperatures and driving forces at each stroke have been tackled as a reliable alternative for the construction of engine setups. However, they can behave very inefficiently depending on the driving used for the work source and/or when temperatures of each stage are very different from each other. Inspired by some models for molecular motors and recent experimental studies, a coupling between driving and velocities is introduced and detail investigated from stochastic thermodynamics. Exact expressions for thermodynamic quantities and distinct maximization routes have been obtained. The search of an optimal coupling provides a substantial increase of engine performance (mainly efficiency), even for large \(\Delta T\) . A simple and general argument for the optimal coupling can be estimated, irrespective of the driving and other model details.




Iago Mamede, Pedro E. Harunari, Bruno A. N. Akasaki, Karel Proesmans and C.E. Fiore
Phys. Rev. E 105, 024106 – Published 4 February, 2022
DOI | arXiv
We introduce an alternative route for obtaining reliable cyclic engines, based on two interacting Brownian particles under time-periodic drivings which can be used as a work-to-work converter or a heat engine. Exact expressions for the thermodynamic fluxes, such as power and heat, are obtained using the framework of stochastic thermodynamic. We then use these exact expression to optimize the driving protocols with respect to output forces, their phase difference. For the work-to-work engine, they are solely expressed in terms of Onsager coefficients and their derivatives, whereas nonlinear effects start to play a role since the particles are at different temperatures. Our results suggest that stronger coupling generally leads to better performance, but careful design is needed to optimize the external forces.

title: Publications
text:
Iago N Mamede, Prashant Singh, Arnab Pal, C.E. Fiore and Karel Proesmans New Journal of Physics, Volume 26, March 2024
DOI | arXiv

title: Projects
text: Microscopic engines operate in a regime where fluctuations are unavoidable. In this project, I investigate how interactions between many microscopic degrees of freedom modify heat currents, power output and efficiency.

title: Projects
text: Microscopic engines operate in a regime where fluctuations are unavoidable. In this project, I investigate how interactions between many microscopic degrees of freedom modify heat currents, power output and efficiency.

title: Projects
text:
Nonequilibrium Ising and Potts systems
I use interacting spin models coupled to multiple reservoirs to study phase transitions, stationary distributions and thermodynamic currents far from equilibrium.

title: Projects
text:
Large deviations of heat fluctuations
I study rare heat fluctuations using variational methods, stochastic calculus and large-deviation theory.

title: Projects
text:
Brownian machines under nonlinear driving
I model Brownian particles driven by nonlinear time-dependent forces to understand power, heat and irreversibility.

title: Research
text: My research is centered on stochastic thermodynamics: the theory of heat, work and entropy production in systems where thermal fluctuations are not negligible.

title: Research
text:
Stochastic thermodynamics
I study thermodynamic quantities at the trajectory level. In this framework, heat, work and entropy production are fluctuating observables accumulated along individual realizations of a stochastic process.
For a Markov jump process, the entropy production rate can be written as
\[
\dot{\Sigma}
=
\sum_{i<j}
J_{ij}
\ln
\frac{w_{ij}p_j}{w_{ji}p_i},
\]
where \(J_{ij}\) is the probability current between states (i) and (j).

title: Research
text:
Nonequilibrium spin systems
A major part of my work concerns interacting spin systems coupled to multiple thermal reservoirs. These models provide minimal platforms to study how collective effects, phase transitions and thermodynamic performance emerge in nonequilibrium heat engines.

title: Research
text:
Large deviations and rare events
Rare fluctuations often dominate the behavior of nonequilibrium systems. I use large-deviation theory to characterize atypical heat, work and current fluctuations.
The central object is often the scaled cumulant generating function,
\[
\lambda(s)
=
\lim_{t\to\infty}
\frac{1}{t}
\ln
\left\langle
e^{s A_t}
\right\rangle,
\]
where \(A_t\) is a time-integrated observable.

title: Research
text:
Brownian systems and nonlinear driving
I also investigate Brownian systems under nonlinear forces and time-dependent protocols, focusing on heat flows, power output and entropy production.

title: Contact
text: I am happy to discuss stochastic thermodynamics, nonequilibrium statistical physics, scientific visualization and possible collaborations.

title: Iago Mamede
text:
I study how microscopic systems exchange heat, perform work and produce entropy under the influence of fluctuations.
My research combines stochastic thermodynamics, interacting spin models, Brownian dynamics and large-deviation theory to understand energy conversion, irreversibility and collective behavior far from equilibrium.






Heat, work and entropy production at the level of stochastic trajectories.



Collective behavior in interacting systems coupled to multiple reservoirs.



Rare fluctuations, tilted generators and conditioned stochastic dynamics.





At microscopic scales, thermodynamic quantities are not simply numbers assigned to macroscopic states. Heat, work and entropy production fluctuate from one trajectory to another.
This is the regime where stochastic thermodynamics becomes essential.

title: About
text:
I am a physicist working on stochastic thermodynamics and nonequilibrium statistical physics.
My research focuses on microscopic systems where fluctuations are essential, such as stochastic heat engines, interacting spin models and Brownian systems.
I am particularly interested in how heat, work and entropy production emerge at the trajectory level, and how collective effects and rare fluctuations shape the behavior of systems far from equilibrium.

text:
